document.source_title: Saudi Arabia: 1.5 million join Hajj pilgrimage despite war
document.summary: This year, Saudi Arabia is proceeding with the Hajj pilgrimage amidst ongoing conflict, expecting around 1.5 million pilgrims. The event, which runs from May 25 to May 29, is complicated by the Iran war and travel advisories from several countries. Despite these challenges, many pilgrims remain committed to their plans, driven by religious duty and long-term preparations.
key_takeaways: 1.5 million pilgrims expected for Hajj 2026 despite ongoing war.
Hajj runs from May 25 to May 29, 2026.
Travel advisories issued by the US and European nations.
Pilgrims motivated by religious duty, with high cancellation hurdles.
Economic impacts include rising costs and insurance challenges.
Iranian pilgrims expected to be around 30,000 this year.
document.why_it_matters: The Hajj pilgrimage is a significant religious event for Muslims worldwide, and its continuation amidst conflict highlights the resilience of faith and the complexities of logistics and safety in such circumstances.

claims.claim_text: Saudi Arabia is proceeding with the Hajj pilgrimage amidst ongoing conflict.
claims.evidence_text: This year is the first time that Saudi Arabia has chosen to go ahead with the annual Hajj pilgrimage while it is also in the middle of a war.

claims.claim_text: Travel advisories have been issued by the US and European nations.
claims.evidence_text: 2026 also marks the first time the US government has urged its own citizens to reconsider participating in the pilgrimage.

claims.claim_text: Economic impacts are affecting the cost of the pilgrimage.
claims.evidence_text: The pilgrimage is costing more. Jet fuel prices have risen and although governments in countries like India, Indonesia and Malaysia often negotiate flight tickets to Hajj, not all have covered cost increases demanded by airlines.
